cataloging_subject.label: A Generall Rule to Teche Euery Man That Is Willynge for to Lerne, to Serve a Lorde or Mayster in Euery Thyng to His Plesure
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The manuscript-defined Middle English household-service tract in British Library Add MS 37969, fols. 2r-8r. Its three consecutively transmitted rubricated components cover service to a lord, the order of fish dishes, and a gentleman carver's conduct; the single closing explicit and the British Library's unitary catalog description support treating them as integral sections of one tract. The record does not describe the manuscript's flyleaf memoranda, medical recipes on fol. 8v, the whole 1914 composite EETS volume, its Franciscan works, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: A Generall Rule to Teche Euery Man That Is Willynge for to Lerne, to Serve a Lorde or Mayster in Euery Thyng to His Plesure
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composition undated; first attested at the end of the fifteenth century
catalog_summary.region: England; exact composition and copying place unknown

titles.value: Generall Rule to Teche Euery Man That Is Willynge for to Lerne, to Serve a Lorde or Mayster in Euery Thyng to His Plesure

agents.name: R. W. Chambers
notes: Editor, transcriber, and commentator for the courtesy-book component of EETS Original Series 148.
No author is named. The reviewed evidence does not establish whether one person composed or assembled all three integral sections.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous scribe of British Library Add MS 37969

scope.description: Copying of the household-service tract at fols. 2r-8r

scope.description: Transcription from British Museum Additional MS 37969, now British Library Add MS 37969
Introduction, notes, and glossary in the 1914 edition; omitted from the U-M encoding

date_statements.display: No later than the end of the fifteenth century; otherwise undated
notes: The manuscript date supplies a terminus ante quem for the text represented by this copy, not an exact composition date.

date_statements.display: British Library Add MS 37969, end of the fifteenth century

notes: The Middle English language and English household context support a broad English cultural origin. Talaton, Devon, appears only in separate flyleaf wood memoranda and is not evidence for composition or copying place.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The lone Latin rubric marker Nota is too slight to justify a Latin work-level language facet. Latin medical recipes on fol. 8v are outside the cataloged work.

form_statements.description: Instructional prose in three consecutively transmitted sections; the repository body contains no verse-line elements.

subject_statements.label: Household food service and course organization

summaries.value: An anonymous late-medieval Middle English tract governing service in a great household. Its principal section assigns duties and authority to the marshal, usher, steward, sewer, carver, almoner, pantry and buttery staff, and other servants before, during, and after meals; two shorter concluding sections order the service of fish dishes and instruct a gentleman carver in handling a towel, food, posture, and deference.

The British Library catalogs the text at Add MS 37969 fols. 2r-8r as one Generall Rule and includes the fish rule within that description. Chambers likewise calls the transmitted sequence one tract and prints its three rubricated sections continuously, followed by the sole explicit. The sections are therefore represented as integral components rather than three independently attested works or an editorial selection.

extent.description: One manuscript-defined prose tract with three integral sections. The repository XML BODY contains 3 DIV1 elements and 20 P elements (18 + 1 + 1), not 25 body paragraphs; the whole XML has 25 P because 5 occur in the header. The body also preserves 7 print-page breaks, 12 manuscript-leaf milestones, one Latin Nota marker, one deletion, and no verse-line elements. These are representation metrics, not counts of separate works.

content_parts.label: Household-service tract
content_parts.summary: The complete tract on household service, fish-course order, and the conduct of a gentleman carver.
notes: The parent carries the one unique work count; child sections carry zero additional items to avoid double counting.

content_parts.label: Rule for serving a lord or master
content_parts.summary: Defines the duties, hierarchy, conduct, meal service, provisioning, lodging, accounting, and disciplinary authority of great-household officers and servants.
notes: The encoding represents this section with 18 body paragraphs.

content_parts.label: Rule for serving fish
content_parts.summary: Orders preserved, fried, sea, river, pond, roasted, shell, baked, and delicate fish dishes across successive courses.

content_parts.label: Rule for a gentleman carver
content_parts.summary: Instructs a gentleman carver in towel placement, carrying and presenting food, kneeling or standing according to the lord's posture, and formal obedience.
notes: The manuscript-defined tract ends after this section with Explicit a good techyng.

evidence.locator: HEADER; FRONT title page; BODY sections RuleServeLd:1-3
evidence.summary: Identifies publication item RuleServLd/RuleServeLd, the 1914 Chambers source, Add MS 37969, U-M's 1999 ScanWorx OCR process, electronic omissions and silent expansion practice, provider terms, three encoded sections, and the complete selected Middle English text.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection and browse listing for RuleServeLd.
evidence.locator: Browse entry for Generall rule; collection access and rights statement
evidence.summary: The current collection publicly lists RuleServeLd with Chambers, Add MS 37969, and EETS OS 148, provides collection access, and labels collection materials public domain in the United States under No Copyright-United States.
notes: The exact item and name-resolver routes returned an automated Cloudflare challenge during this review; the official browse listing and repository artifact supplied the item evidence.

evidence.citation: R. W. Chambers and Walter W. Seton, eds., A Fifteenth-Century Courtesy Book and Two Fifteenth-Century Franciscan Rules, EETS Original Series 148 (London, 1914), Internet Archive courtesybook00unknuoft.
evidence.locator: Contents; Chambers's component title page; introduction pp. 3-8; text pp. 11-17; notes pp. 18-22; glossary p. 125
evidence.summary: The inspected complete scan establishes Chambers's responsibility, his singular treatment of the Generall Rule as the first tract, Add MS 37969 as the source, the continuously printed three-section text and sole explicit, editorial comparison, notes, and glossary.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, A Generall Rule, Add MS 37969, record 032-002054449.
evidence.locator: Work description, contents, date, language, physical description, access, and Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: The current catalog describes one late-fifteenth-century Generall Rule for household officers at fols. 2r-8r, expressly includes the fish rule within it, records the single colophon Explicit a good techyng, and distinguishes the flyleaf memoranda and fol. 8v medical recipes from the work.

evidence.citation: XPath and element-count inspection of repository RuleServLd.xml performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: Counts 3 BODY DIV1 elements, 20 BODY P elements distributed 18 + 1 + 1, 7 BODY PB elements, 12 BODY manuscript-leaf MILESTONE elements, one FOREIGN element containing Latin Nota, one DEL, no L elements, and 25 P elements across the whole XML because 5 occur in the header.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for RuleServLd.
evidence.summary: Binds the work record to Add MS 37969, Chambers's 1914 edition, U-M's 1999 encoding, the local XML artifact, the complete 1914 edition scan, and current access and rights evidence.

open_questions.question: Did the fish-service and carver sections circulate independently before their transmission with the principal household-service rule?
next_steps: Consult a current census and scholarship on Middle English household and courtesy texts.
Retain the three parts as integral sections of the attested Add MS 37969 tract unless independent witnesses or source relations are identified.

open_questions.question: When, where, and by whom was the household-service tract composed or compiled?
next_steps: Consult current linguistic, paleographical, and household-literature scholarship.
Do not convert the manuscript date or Talaton flyleaf memoranda into a precise composition date or place.

notes: The publication-set filename and work ID are RuleServLd, while the XML IDNO and all DIV1 NODE values are RuleServeLd; both forms are preserved with their layer-specific scopes.

The XML source-description note Derived from olim Seton, olim Pennant (whereabouts unknown) was inherited from the Franciscan portion of the composite 1914 carrier and is not provenance evidence for British Library Add MS 37969.

The XML PROFILEDESC declares only Latin, but direct text inspection shows Middle English throughout apart from the single rubric marker Nota; the manifest therefore records only Middle English at work level.
Rights, provider terms, reproduction access, and the current absence of a verified public complete manuscript facsimile are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
